en.extract:
The black-eared barbet (Psilopogon duvaucelii) is a barbet in the family Megalaimidae native to Peninsular Malaysia, Sumatra and Borneo. It inhabits shrubland and forest up to an altitude of 1,200 m (3,900 ft). Because of its large range it is listed as least concern on the IUCN Red List.


Characteristics
The black-eared barbet has olive-green plumage, a blue throat and nape, a black beak and a large black streak between the eyes. Below the eyes it has red spots encircled by black.
It is 16–17 cm (6.3–6.7 in) long and weighs 26.3–37 g (0.93–1.31 oz).


Taxonomy
Bucco duvaucelii was the scientific name proposed by René Lesson in 1830 who described a barbet from Sumatra.
The generic name Psilopogon was proposed by Salomon Müller in 1836, initially for a fire-tufted barbet (P. pyrolophus) from Sumatra.
In subsequent decades, about 19 generic names were proposed for barbet species, including Megalaima by John Edward Gray in 1849 and Mezobucco by George Ernest Shelley in 1889. Taxonomists used different generic names when describing barbets in collections of natural history museums.
Mezzobucco duvaucelii gigantorhinus was proposed by Harry C. Oberholser in 1912 for an adult male barbet with a large bill from Nias Island.
Mezzobucco duvaucelii tanamassae was proposed by Rodolphe Meyer de Schauensee in 1929 for a male barbet from the Batu Islands that differed from M. d. gigantorhinus by a more extensive red colour on the breast.
Three black-eared barbet subspecies are recognized as valid taxa since 2014:

P. d. duvaucelii – the nominate subspecies; Peninsular Malaysia, Sumatra, Borneo and Bangka Island
P. d. gigantorhinus – Nias Island, off western Sumatra
P. d. tanamassae – Batu Islands, off western Sumatra


References


External links
BirdLife International (2019). "Black-eared Barbet Psilopogon duvaucelii".

The '''black-eared barbet''' ('''''Psilopogon duvaucelii''''') is a barbet in the family [[Megalaimidae]] native to [[Peninsular Malaysia]], [[Sumatra]] and [[Borneo]]. It inhabits shrubland and forest up to an altitude of {{convert|1200|m|ft|abbr=on}}. Because of its large range it is listed as [[least concern]] on the [[IUCN Red List]].<ref name=iucn/>

== Characteristics ==
The black-eared barbet has olive-green plumage, a blue throat and nape, a black beak and a large black streak between the eyes. Below the eyes it has red spots encircled by black.<ref name=Lesson1831>{{cite book |last1=Lesson |first1=R. P. |year=1831 |title=Traité d'ornithologie, ou Tableau méthodique des ordres, sous-ordres, familles, tribus, genres, sous-genres et races d'oiseau |volume=Tome 1 |location=Strasbourg |publisher=F. G. Levrault |chapter=Barbu de Duvaucel; Bucco Duvaucelii |page=164 |chapter-url=http://docnum.u-strasbg.fr/cdm/compoundobject/collection/coll13/id/133436/show/132935}}</ref>
It is {{convert|16|-|17|cm|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|26.3|-|37|g|abbr=on}}.<ref name=HBW2014>{{cite book |last1=del Hoyo, J. |last2=Collar, N. J. |last3=Kirwan, G. M. |date=2014 |title=Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International Illustrated Checklist of the Birds of the World |volume=7: Jacamars to Woodpeckers |editor=del Hoyo, J. |editor2=Elliott, A. |editor3=Sargatal, J. |editor4=Christie, D.A. |editor5=de Juana, E. |publisher=Lynx Edicions and BirdLife International |location=Barcelona, Spain and Cambridge, UK |chapter=Black-eared Barbet ''Psilopogon duvaucelii'' |chapter-url=https://www.hbw.com/ibc/species/black-eared-barbet-psilopogon-duvaucelii}}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
''Bucco duvaucelii'' was the [[scientific name]] proposed by [[René Lesson]] in 1830 who [[scientific description|described]] a barbet from Sumatra.<ref name=Lesson1831/>
The generic name ''Psilopogon'' was proposed by [[Salomon Müller]] in 1836, initially for a [[fire-tufted barbet]] (''P. pyrolophus'') from Sumatra.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Müller |first1=S. |year=1835 |title=Aanteekeningen over de natuurlijke gesteldheid van een gedeelte der westkust en binnenlanden van Sumatra, met bijvoeging van eenige waarnemingen en beschrijvingen van verscheid dieren |journal=Tijdschrift voor Natuurlijke Geschiedenis en Physiologie |volume=2 |pages=315–355 |url=https://books.google.com/books?id=XERnAAAAcAAJ&pg=PA339}}</ref>
In subsequent decades, about 19 generic names were proposed for barbet species, including ''Megalaima'' by [[John Edward Gray]] in 1849 and ''Mezobucco'' by [[George Ernest Shelley]] in 1889. Taxonomists used different generic names when describing barbets in collections of [[natural history museums]].<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Ripley |first1=S. D. |year=1945 |title=The barbets |journal=The Auk |volume=62 |issue=4 |pages=542–563 |doi=10.2307/4079804 |jstor=4079804 |url=https://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/auk/v062n04/p0542-p0563.pdf}}</ref>

''Mezzobucco duvaucelii gigantorhinus'' was proposed by [[Harry C. Oberholser]] in 1912 for an adult male barbet with a large bill from [[Nias]] Island.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Oberholser |first1=H. C. |year=1912 |title=Descriptions of one hundred and four new species and subspecies of birds from the Barussan islands and Sumatra |journal=Smithsonian Miscellaneous Collections |volume=60 |issue=7 |pages=1–22 |url=https://repository.si.edu/bitstream/handle/10088/23487/SMC_60_Oberholser_1912_7_1-22.pdf}}</ref>
''Mezzobucco duvaucelii tanamassae'' was proposed by [[Rodolphe Meyer de Schauensee]] in 1929 for a male barbet from the [[Batu Islands]] that differed from ''M. d. gigantorhinus'' by a more extensive red colour on the breast.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Meyer de Schauensee |first1=R. |year=1929 |title=A New Barbet from the Batu Islands |journal=Proceedings of the Academy of Natural Sciences of Philadelphia |volume=81 |page=521 |jstor=4064046}}</ref>

Three black-eared barbet [[subspecies]] are recognized as [[Valid name (zoology)|valid]] [[taxa]] since 2014:<ref name=HBW2014/>
*''P. d. duvaucelii'' – the nominate subspecies; Peninsular Malaysia, Sumatra, Borneo and [[Bangka Island]]
*''P. d. gigantorhinus'' – Nias Island, off western Sumatra		
*''P. d. tanamassae'' – Batu Islands, off western Sumatra
